author: @TexasWaters
text:
Great Videos !
Question Sir, What do you like more, the X1C or the X-Max 3? I like the heated camber and the bed on the Max but the X1C seems more refined and user friendly. Just wished the X1C had he heated chamber and the bed that's on the Max.
I don't understand why companies build printers with enclosures and promote they can print ABS, ASA, PVA, PET
Ideal for PA, PC, Carbon/ Glass Fiber Reinforced Polymer and don't install a active heater, maybe I don't get it.

Thanks !

text: Its a delightful printer in my experience, had pinters since I built my own 15yrs ago and this is the first no hassle printer I've owned. I've not sent it anything too wacky, but I think I've only had one failure and it was probably my fault getting a little frugal with supports. Great to see the video, really enjoyed.

author: @chiperchap
text: That walled garden thing always sets something back IMHO Rob. But the emergence of metal and carbon printing. Been playing around with silver and copper clay that you then 'torch' to cast it. Akin to mazack? Dunno if i spelled that right. But some of the metal printers I've seen that are basically a mig welder on a cnc. The ability to print small metal and ceramic at home is already a reality! Wow! I'd love to see a video on that Rob if you think it would be interesting

author: @MichaelStoko
text: Thank you Dr. RMS! Glad to see you are back at it! We all really appreciate the invaluable treasure trove of knowledge, tech, and tools for self-empowerment you've put together on this channel. There's no limit to the amount of good it can do, so thank you once again for your kindness and generosity in sharing your builds, and also for your determination, perseverance and the inhuman work ethic you employ to keep pumping out vids! It is truly inspirational, check one for inspiration, in fact - I have to get building right now. Thank you!

author: @Iandefor
text: Robert, I was so sorry to hear about your recent loss. Been an avid watcher for a few months now, and tbh I feel like your channel has been life-changing. It's good to see you uploading again. May the loss become ever easier to bear.

author: @joefarr3304
text: I've used cheaper printers since they first appeared and for the last 3 years been using a Creality printer which was ok. I was then persuaded to get a Bambu A1 and it's completely on another level. Even though the A1 goes through a lengthy cleaning and levelling setup at the start of each print, it's 3 to 4 times faster than the Creality and the print quality is many many times better. This is all running at the printers 100% setting. There is a faster speed that I've not tried. I also tell the printer I'm using Bambu filament because apparently the printer runs faster, when in reality I'm using a generic brand from Amazon. I've had it for around a month so early days but it's truly an excellent printer.

author: @TheMrReactorcore
text:
Very cool series you're doing!
Having made graphene and having tried mixing it with plastic, do you think diy graphene filament might work? I know there are solutions for diy filament making and commercial actors making graphene filament, but i'm not sure how the graphene might change the properties of the filament (shapeability, melting temperature etc). Ratios and efficient ways to mix the graphene into the filament might be interesting to test? Could you use graphene instead of copper to 3d print a fully working generator without the need for copper coils?

I also want to say a huge thank you for these videos! I've been in a slump for years, but after watching quite a few of your videos, i've invested in a bit of equipment and think just testing ideas and learning might be a really rewarding hobby in of itself. 
All the best from Norway.

author: @Kungpaoshizzi
text: I bought this thing for the lidar, I will never buy another printer that doesn't come with the lidar. I've gone through idk how many countless rolls and haven't had to tinker once. PETG required a minus .03mm, but that's expected given PETG's sticky nature.

author: @blackseabrew
text:
So grateful for your return and so sorry for your loss!!

I love my Bambu printer. It printed right out of the box. I can typically find the design I need online and download it. One popular item I've printed is battery-powered tool holders that screw into a wall. Family and friends love them to keep their power tools and batteries on the wall and not on their bench. I purchased a small variety of different resins. The matte-finish material definitely requires an extra 20 degrees C temp increase for good layer adherance. Getting ready to design/print a piece that typically fails in MIG welder wire feed mechanisms. The manufacturers don't sell them so why not?

author: @mythrail
text:
Not related to the video, just wanted to put it out somewhere that people might see it.
On Concentrating solar power (CSP) / Photovoltaics

I wonder if you could figure out a material that reflects the parts of the spectrum that photovoltaics can't absorb to a central collector.
I also wonder if you could concentrate enough heat to be useful with a cascading heat pump arrangement, and whether or not you could get to the temperatures that are useful for a CSP.
If the heat pumps extract heat from/around the solar panels themselves it could even optimize the efficiency of the photovoltaics.
This would probably cause condensation, which could mean you could harvest water from the air.
I'd imagine that would cause convection to happen, I'd also want to figure out if you could harvest energy from the movement of the air in the same footprint.

My guess is that multiple points along the way would have issues (which may be why it isn't done), but I always wonder why I rarely hear people dreaming about optimizing a system as a whole / rethinking the system as a whole.

author: @calebphelps7030
text: You can also use Orca slicer for your x1 carbon......and for all your other printers as well. Its an open source build that builds on the bambu studio platform with more options. Next step up after this for you would be an SLS machine.....but they are still quite expensive, but it gets you into printing higher temp resistant materials much easier and is also what most metal 3d printers use as well. If you need any of your own models printed in a metal, pcbway offers the service and the parts are great.....i just had them print a key that I modeled up for a customer out of aluminum after i had ensured it worked properly with a plastic version first. Rapid prototyping at its finest.....love 3 printing! Cheers

author: @wheelsdan
text:
Hi Rob great advice as always and lovely to see patii in the picture .
I'm looking into a bamboo lab printer as I'm starting a scuba servicing company . What's 3d printing got to do with scuba diving you say... well loads really the dive industry had gone massive with 3d printing.  Why I would use one is to make tools ! I want to make a vacuum test machine to test cracking pressure on scuba regulators and even tools to take regs apart without damaging them with metal tools. My problem is to get a good one they cost a fortune! Last time I looked £1400 for the bamboo x1 carbon... please could you advise what is best out there for quality and speed ?
Thanks mate

author: @CraigLandsberg-lk1ep
text: I hate computers and any device with a chip😢 I have hundreds of devices in my house, that died one day after the warranty ran out, and i might have only used them 3x in that period. I am an engineer/sheetmetal worker and I call it 'disposable engineering'😮 they put a chip in it just to make it vulnerable, and once the remote dies there isn't enough buttons on the machine to use it! I can't be the only one who thinks this. And mobile phones in the '90's were much more reliable than now, the whole screen didn't shatter when you dropped it! They deliberately make phones thin no 'so'  they break😢 Modern technology is pathetic¡ I only believe in mechanical technology 😅 I feel like I am going crazy. Like Will Smith, I am also a malfunction magnet¿ But at least the Author is showing me how to salvage useful parts from some of it😅 i have pulled 2 LCD TV's apart the other day and have 4 more to go, but I didn't know we could repurpose the screen sections. I have salvaged the best part of them, which is the huge chunk of aluminium that is the 'chasis' of modern TV's, as well as lots metal angle, transformers, ally heatsinks, hundreds of screws and lots of electrical wire and more. Thanks Robert😅😅 sorry for my rant, but I thought you might be the only person who might understand my anxiety, since I am now disabled and can't afford to peplace hardly any of it. Have had 3 TV's and 3 phones die just in the last 12mths and am loosing my mind!¡ But your video's have given me a new lease on life, and some days a reason to live 😮 thankyou so much, and I look forward to getting through all your videos, from Queensland Australia 🌏😀😀

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: we have become a consumerist disposabe society that is for sure - change is coming but it is slow

author: @johnprouty6583
text: Good review!  I’ve had mine for a few months now and it really is easy to use.  It does require periodic maintenance including regularly cleaning your build plates.  I found to really get the most out of your printer that you should learn a fair bit of CAD so you can design your own parts.  The benefit is that it naturally takes you to another discipline where you are continually learning.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @spehropefhany
text: I like this printer. The only issues I've had are with (non-Bambu, primarily) filament breaking inside the AMS or the feed to the extruder. Taking it apart to remove the fragments is a bit time consuming and there is plenty of opportunity to damage the fine wires in the print head and the AMS mechanism if you're not very careful. On the plus side, the print quality is markedly better than my 7-year old dual extruder machine, and the speed is also better. No comparison to a low-end Ender machine. My expectations are high since I paid full price for the machine and I already had a couple printers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Yes I was thinking of using non Bambu filament and just getting it working on the LAN was a bit of a learning curve lol - so I am expecting the same deal for using anything 'outside of the garden'!

author: @vivisector3489
text:
I got myself a P1P last year and I've been very happy with it. 
Bambu Studio is an open source fork of PrusaSlicer, so the ecosystem of Bambu isn't really as closed as you make it sound, in my opinion.
I bet they did envision becoming 'the apple of 3d printing' though, at least I can see it in their advertisement and their strategy.
But you can buy any part of the printed from their site for quite reasonable price and, as you said, there are 3rd party "hacks" like print beds and nozzles out there.
It's really a shame how the 3d printing community is treating all the new-comers that are asking "stupid questions" when they hit a snag in print quality or a problem with their printer.
I do understand why though, so many old-school 3d printing nerds have fought and tinkered with their printers, getting their settings and mods just right for perfect prints, and now anyone can buy a Bambu Labs printer and get really top notch prints right out of the box, until they see something they don't understand and they have to ask for help for an issue they can't even put into correct words. I guess X1 Carbon comes with the engineering plate I'm not sure, but I've had absolutely no problems with print adhesion on my powder coated textured PEI plate (as long as I keep it clean).

author: @lv_woodturner3899
text:
Other YouTube videos have shown using Cura, PrusaSlicer, Orca Sliver, etc.   The nuance is needing to create and tweak profiles in the other slicers to get the same quality print and print speed as with the Bambu slicer.
This is a nice piece of kit but too rich for me.   I purchased a Qidi-Tech X-Plus 3.   I have been happy with this machine.   I have the same use-it-out-of-the-box experience.   I love the ease or connecting it to my local network wifi, no apps, just type in my local network address in the Device tab of the slicer and it connects.

author: @RocketIIIman
text: Robert, switch to OrcaSlicer, its a fork of Bambu Studio but includes updates from other slicers too, and it works with most other printers too.

author: @jamesross1003
text: Looks pretty simple and would probably make a good starter for beginners to use.  Which I am guessing is the original thought behind it.  So sounds like they pretty much meet what expectations that they have set.  Thanks for the video Rob!  Keep on keeping on!

author: @christianlainesse4281
text: now all we need is CAD software that's just as easy to use (or at least easy to learn)

replies.author: @spehropefhany
replies.text: I don't see an "ease of use" issue. I see a $$$ issue. There is no good open-source 3D parametric modeling software, an equivalent of Gimp, KiCAD or Inkscape.

replies.author: @colleenforrest7936
replies.text:
AI CAD! 
The nuances of prompt writing to get the exact output you want could prove to be almost as intricate as programming though, at least at this stage of the commercial AIs.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: try tinkercad mate - it is pretty easy to start with and makes a great 'jumping off' point into the world of 3D design
